name: background-process
description: Claude runs a Bash command with run_in_background:true (`sleep 8 && echo done`); the tool returns immediately carrying toolUseResult.backgroundTaskId, then the agent polls the process via the BashOutput tool until it reports a terminated status. WHAT it captures: the user-observable arc that PR #450 (issue #445) added — ready → working at the prompt, then the session STAYS working past the turn's own end_turn while the detached shell process is alive, then a single working → ready once the process exits. WHY this exact shape: #450 detects the spawn from the structured backgroundTaskId and gates IsAgentDone on a live `lsof` probe of the tasks/<id>.output file, so the only way to make the daemon observe completion (rather than just letting the turn end) is to drive a BashOutput poll that reports `completed` — there is no async exit hook the tailer can see. The single prompt asks claude to spawn AND poll-to-completion in one turn so the driver's one wait_turn brackets the whole arc. HOW it differs between runs: how many BashOutput polls the model issues, exact token counts, and the precise working dwell length vary with model pacing — the spec's dwell assertion is a ≥4s lower bound (well inside the 8s sleep) so jitter never trips it; the structural arc (one ready→working, one working→ready, single session) is fixed. WHERE to look if it breaks: if the session flips to ready while the process is still alive, the daemon's lsof probe or backgroundTaskId extraction regressed (core/application/services/background_probe.go, core/adapters/inbound/agents/claudecode/parser.go); if the session never returns to ready, the BashOutput-completed parse or the refreshStaleSessions re-probe regressed.

metadata.notes:
agent_supports: yes
daemon_capability: full; driver_capability: ready

assessment.body:
## Verdict

agent_supports: yes
daemon_capability: full; driver_capability: ready

## Re-evaluation (2026-05-23, post-#445)

This supersedes the 2026-05-18 / earlier 2026-05-23 verdict of yes/no. The gap is now CLOSED: irrlicht tracks Bash background processes and holds the session working until they exit. Two landed changes, both present in this worktree (branch feat/record-background-process-fixture):

1. **#450 (commit b8d8e2dc) — background-process tracking.** The parser snapshots each background process from the structured `toolUseResult.backgroundTaskId` (+ its `tasks/<id>.output` path) and surfaces it as `SessionMetrics.BackgroundProcessCount`. The daemon gates `IsAgentDone` on `HasLiveBackgroundProcess`, holding the session `working` past `end_turn` until the process exits; an `lsof` probe on the output file is the authoritative liveness check, and a `BashOutput`-terminated status or `KillShell` drops the process.

2. **265ebf22 — transcript-level completion via `<task-notification>`.** The #450 completion path was written against a `queued_command` attachment shape; a claude launched under the Agent SDK harness (exactly what irrlicht's recording agent uses) suppresses BashOutput/KillShell and instead reports completion via TaskOutput + a terminal `<task-notification>` whose `<task-id>` is the `backgroundTaskId`. 265ebf22 teaches the parser to drop the tracked process on that terminal notification (both on-disk shapes: the `origin.kind` user line AND the queued_command attachment), keyed on the same `openBackgroundProcs` map. This makes the scenario recordable from inside the SDK harness — the daemon observes the transcript-level completion and the session settles `working → ready` with no env-unset needed.

## How it was validated

Recorded against a live `claude` v2.1.150 driven through the committed recipe, observed by an `irrlichd` built from THIS worktree (version `0.4.7+265ebf2`, includes 265ebf22). The captured transcript carries: a `Bash` tool_use with `run_in_background:true` (`sleep 8 && echo BG_DONE`), a `toolUseResult.backgroundTaskId`, BashOutput polls reporting `<status>completed</status>`, and the terminal `<task-notification>`. The events.jsonl shows the exact spec arc for the primary session: `ready` at birth (+21 ms), `ready → working` on the prompt, an extended `working` dwell of ~25.3 s with NO intermediate transition to ready/waiting while the bg process was alive, then a single `working → ready` once it completed. `expected-validate` passes 5/5 phases.

## Why this is a clean yes (not partial)

The transcript-level completion path means the daemon no longer depends solely on the lsof liveness fallback to release the session — the SDK-harness `<task-notification>` is an actual transcript signal that decrements the count and re-classifies. All three user-observable spec signals are now real: `BackgroundProcessCount > 0` while alive, session held `working` past end_turn, and a single `working → ready` once the last bg process exits.
caveats: agent_supports is yes — confirmed against the live claude v2.1.150 binary: run_in_background Bash parameter + BashOutput + KillShell + backgroundTaskId all present in the strings scan.

irrlicht_observes flipped no → yes: #450 (b8d8e2dc) added BackgroundProcessCount + the working-hold gated on HasLiveBackgroundProcess, and 265ebf22 added recognition of the terminal <task-notification> completion shape the SDK-harnessed claude actually emits. Validated 5/5 by a fresh recording from a daemon built from this worktree (0.4.7+265ebf2).

The recording was captured in isolated mode (run-cell.sh without --attach) so the observing daemon was built from THIS worktree (265ebf22), not the user's separately-running daemon. Daemon version baked into manifest.json is 0.4.7+265ebf2.

Re-record variance: exact working dwell length and BashOutput poll count vary with model pacing; the spec's bg_alive_working assertion is a ≥4s lower bound well inside the 8s sleep, so timing jitter never trips it.

sources.note: Adds background-process tracking: parser snapshots backgroundTaskId into BackgroundProcessCount; daemon gates IsAgentDone on HasLiveBackgroundProcess (lsof liveness probe on tasks/<id>.output) to hold the session working past end_turn; BashOutput-terminated / KillShell drop the process.

Recognizes the SDK-harness completion shape: a terminal <task-notification> (both the origin.kind user-line shape and the queued_command attachment shape) whose <task-id> == backgroundTaskId drops the tracked process from the open set, so the session settles working→ready via a transcript signal — no lsof-only dependency, replay-compatible. This is what makes the scenario recordable from inside the recording agent's own SDK harness.
Fresh recording: daemon_version 0.4.7+265ebf2 (built from this worktree, includes 265ebf22), expected_pass_rate 5/5 phases passed.

5/5 phases pass: session_birth (ready +21ms), pid_bind (+21ms), turn_start (ready→working), bg_alive_working (~25.3s working dwell, no transition to ready/waiting while alive), turn_end (working→ready at +25265ms, under the 60s max and after the 8s sleep).
Confirms agent_supports: yes — claude v2.1.150 ships run_in_background (Bash param), backgroundTaskId (34 hits), BashOutput (14 hits), KillShell (8 hits).

preconditions: claude CLI on PATH and authenticated (v2.1.x ships the run_in_background Bash parameter plus the BashOutput and KillShell tools)

irrlichd is recording (--attach or isolated) and built from a tree that includes PR #450 — the daemon must have BackgroundProcessCount tracking and the lsof liveness probe, else it cannot observe the background process at all
lsof is on PATH (the daemon's authoritative background-process liveness check shells out to `lsof -t` on each tasks/<id>.output file)
setup: Driver launches claude in a fresh trusted cwd under <staging>/cwd as usual; no seed files needed — the background process is a self-contained `sleep && echo`.

script.text: Do exactly this and nothing else. (1) Use the Bash tool to run the command `sleep 8 && echo BG_DONE` with run_in_background set to true, so it launches in the background and the tool returns immediately. (2) Then repeatedly use the BashOutput tool to poll that background shell, waiting about 2 seconds between polls, until BashOutput reports the process status as completed. (3) Only after BashOutput shows it completed, reply with exactly the word: done. Do not run any other commands.

verify: events.jsonl contains a transcript_new + pid_discovered for the primary session UUID within ~2s of launch (session born in ready)
events.jsonl shows the primary session transition ready -> working when the prompt lands

the primary session stays working for at least ~4s after the turn-start working transition with NO state_transition to ready while the background process is alive — this is the #450 hold-past-end_turn behavior (without #450 the session would flip to ready as soon as the Bash tool_use returns)
transcript.jsonl contains a Bash tool_use whose input has run_in_background:true and a following tool_result whose toolUseResult carries a backgroundTaskId
transcript.jsonl contains one or more BashOutput tool_use blocks, and a BashOutput tool_result whose text reports a terminated status (e.g. <status>completed</status>)
events.jsonl shows exactly one working -> ready transition for the primary session, occurring AFTER the background process exits (not at the bare end_turn)
final session state for the primary session is ready

metadata.notes:
agent_supports: no
daemon_capability: incapable; driver_capability: n/a

assessment.body:
## Verdict

agent_supports: no
daemon_capability: incapable; driver_capability: n/a

## Scenario

background-process: the agent launches a long-running CHILD process that is tracked SEPARATELY from the turn — a `&`-style background task that the turn does not block on — and the session stays `working` while that process is alive, only flipping to `ready` once the background process exits (a later, out-of-band completion). The user-observable signals (per the claudecode anchor): the primary session holds `working` past the bare `end_turn` for as long as the backgrounded process is alive, then makes exactly one `working -> ready` transition AFTER the process exits — the daemon must observe a backgrounded dispatch plus a later, separate completion.

## Agent does NOT support it (agent_supports: no)

opencode's bash tool runs the command SYNCHRONOUSLY to completion within the single tool call: the tool blocks until the command exits and returns its captured output in the same tool_result. There is no `run_in_background`-style parameter that dispatches a child process which outlives the tool call, and no separate later-completion event (opencode has no analogue of claudecode's `BashOutput`/`KillShell` poll over a `tasks/<bash_id>.output` file). A user wanting a daemonized process can only `cmd &` inside one synchronous bash call, which detaches it from opencode entirely — opencode neither tracks it nor emits a later completion the daemon could key on. So there is no backgrounded-process-with-deferred-completion primitive in the agent at all; the turn ends when the (synchronous) tool call returns.

## daemon cannot observe it (daemon_capability: incapable)

Even setting agent support aside, the daemon's entire background-process machinery is bound to the claudecode JSONL-transcript path and is structurally unavailable to opencode's transport:

- The claudecode parser (core/adapters/inbound/agents/claudecode/parser.go) is what recognizes a background dispatch: a Bash `tool_use` with `run_in_background:true` whose sibling `toolUseResult.backgroundTaskId` marks the spawn, and it extracts each backgrounded command's `tasks/<bash_id>.output` path. The daemon then keeps the session pinned `working` by an `lsof`-based liveness probe (core/application/services/background_probe.go `anyLiveOutputWriter`) over exactly those parser-extracted output paths — `lsof -t` listing a holder means the child is alive, an empty result means it exited (issue #445 / PR #450).
- These observables land on `session.SessionMetrics.BackgroundProcessCount` / `BackgroundProcessOutputs`, which are populated ONLY by the JSONL-tailer metrics adapter (core/adapters/outbound/metrics/adapter.go) from the parser's `backgroundTaskId` + output-path extraction.
- opencode is the lone `ProcessOwnedStore` adapter: its `MetricsReader` (`querySessionMetrics` in core/adapters/inbound/agents/opencode/metrics.go) reads the local SQLite store and populates tokens / tasks / tool-call state / cost / context — but NEVER sets `BackgroundProcessCount` or `BackgroundProcessOutputs`, and there is no `backgroundTaskId`/output-file concept in the opencode store to extract. The opencode adapter package contains no background-process code (its only `lsof` mention is a PID-discovery comment in pid.go, unrelated to background liveness).

The daemon therefore has no transport-level way to see a backgrounded-dispatch + later-completion for opencode: the SQLite store records turn-synchronous tool calls only, with no separate output-file the `lsof` probe could watch. daemon_capability is `incapable` (a structural transport gap), not `bug`.

## Catalog drift note

The scenario's `requires` includes `background_process`, which is one of the `requires` ids flagged as MISSING from features.json earlier this sweep (catalog drift). This assessment records only the applicable:false verdict for the opencode cell; it does NOT edit features.json or the coverage rollup (maintainer-owned).

## What would flip this

Both layers would have to change: upstream opencode would need a real backgrounded-process primitive (a `run_in_background`-style dispatch with a later, queryable completion event), AND the opencode adapter / `ProcessOwnedStore` reader would need to surface that dispatch + a liveness signal (an output-file path for the existing `lsof` probe, or an equivalent store-level liveness field) into `BackgroundProcessCount` / `BackgroundProcessOutputs`. Neither exists today.

## Confidence

0.85: the daemon-incapable verdict is read directly from the source — background tracking is wholly in the claudecode parser + the `lsof` probe over parser-extracted `tasks/*.output` paths, and the opencode `MetricsReader` sets neither background field. agent_supports:no rests on opencode's synchronous bash tool (no `run_in_background` analogue, no `BashOutput`-style later-completion poll). Not higher only because opencode's bash semantics are asserted from the adapter/store shape and general opencode behavior rather than a fresh live `cmd &` probe — but that would not change the daemon-incapable conclusion, which is the binding precondition here.
caveats: daemon_capability is incapable, NOT bug: the gap is structural (opencode's ProcessOwnedStore/SQLite transport carries no backgrounded-dispatch + output-file the lsof liveness probe could watch), not a daemon mishandling of a signal it does receive.

`background_process` (the scenario's requires id) is flagged as missing from features.json this sweep (catalog drift) — recorded here as a note only; features.json and the coverage rollup are maintainer-owned and left untouched.

opencode's bash tool is synchronous-to-completion: there is no run_in_background parameter and no BashOutput/KillShell-style later-completion poll, so the agent has no backgrounded-process-with-deferred-completion primitive (agent_supports:no).

sources.note: background dispatch is recognized HERE: Bash tool_use run_in_background:true + sibling toolUseResult.backgroundTaskId; extracts tasks/<bash_id>.output paths. This parser is claudecode-only — opencode does not use the JSONL-tailer path.

anyLiveOutputWriter: the daemon's authoritative background-process liveness check shells out to `lsof -t --` over the parser-extracted output paths. No output paths (opencode) => nothing to probe (issue #445 / PR #450).
BackgroundProcessCount / BackgroundProcessOutputs are populated only by the JSONL-tailer metrics adapter from the claudecode parser output.

opencode MetricsReader (querySessionMetrics) reads the SQLite store and sets tokens/tasks/tool-call/cost/context — never BackgroundProcessCount or BackgroundProcessOutputs; the store has no backgroundTaskId/output-file concept.

opencode Source is ProcessOwnedStore{Reader: metricsReader{}} — the lone structured-store adapter; no background-process code in the package (the pid.go lsof mention is PID discovery, not background liveness).

anchor: the user-observable shape — session holds working past end_turn while the background process is alive, then exactly one working->ready AFTER it exits. opencode has neither the agent primitive nor the daemon transport to produce this.

recipe.notes: applicable: false (daemon_capability=incapable). agent_supports=no — opencode's bash tool runs SYNCHRONOUSLY to completion within the single tool call: there is no run_in_background-style dispatch of a child process that outlives the turn, and no later out-of-band completion event (no BashOutput/KillShell-style poll analogue). daemon_capability=incapable (structural transport gap, NOT a bug): the daemon's entire background-process machinery is bound to the claudecode JSONL path — the parser recognizes a Bash tool_use run_in_background:true + sibling toolUseResult.backgroundTaskId and extracts each tasks/<bash_id>.output path, and background_probe.go's lsof liveness probe (anyLiveOutputWriter, #445/#450) pins the session working over exactly those parser-extracted paths; BackgroundProcessCount/Outputs are populated ONLY by the JSONL-tailer metrics adapter. opencode is the lone ProcessOwnedStore adapter: its MetricsReader (querySessionMetrics, metrics.go) reads the SQLite store for tokens/tasks/tool-call/cost/context but NEVER sets the background fields, and the store carries no backgroundTaskId/output-file the lsof probe could watch — so a backgrounded-dispatch + later-completion is unobservable for opencode at the transport level. CATALOG DRIFT NOTE: the scenario's requires id `background_process` is one flagged missing from features.json this sweep — recorded here as a note only; features.json and the coverage rollup are maintainer-owned and left untouched. No recipe, no recording; pipeline frozen at Stage 1. UNBLOCK would need BOTH a real opencode backgrounded-process primitive (run_in_background-style dispatch + queryable later completion) AND an opencode/ProcessOwnedStore path that surfaces a liveness signal into BackgroundProcessCount/Outputs. See replaydata/agents/opencode/scenarios/background-process/assessment.json (no / incapable / n.a.).

metadata.notes:
agent_supports: no
daemon_capability: n/a; driver_capability: n/a

assessment.body:
## Verdict

agent_supports: no
daemon_capability: n/a; driver_capability: n/a

## Primitive exercised

Background process management (`run_in_background: true` or shell `&`) — the agent can launch a detached shell process and the daemon tracks its liveness *separately from* transcript turn boundaries: `ready → working` at the prompt, `BackgroundProcessCount > 0` while the process is alive, the session stays `working` past the turn's own end, then a single `working → ready` once the process exits.

## Reasoning — agent_supports: no

pi (v0.70.0) has no fire-and-forget background-process primitive. Three independent confirmations:

1. **Tool schema.** pi's built-in `bash` tool (`dist/core/tools/bash.js`) declares exactly two parameters — `command: string` and `timeout?: number`. There is no `run_in_background` / `background` field the model can set. The only knob is a foreground timeout.
2. **Synchronous execution.** `bash.execute()` returns a Promise that resolves only after `waitForChildProcess(child)` reports the child has terminated (it awaits `ops.exec(...)` to completion). The tool result is emitted at process exit, so a bash call always finishes *inside* the turn — there is no detached handle returned to the model to poll later.
3. **`detached: true` is a red herring.** The Step 2.5 strings/source scan surfaced `detached: true` in `createLocalBashOperations` — but it is paired with `trackDetachedChildPid` / `killProcessTree` and exists purely to put the shell in its own process group so pi can SIGKILL the whole tree on timeout or ESC-abort. It is process-group hygiene, not backgrounding; the parent still blocks on exit.

The pi README explicitly disclaims the feature: **"No background bash. Use tmux. Full observability, direct interaction."** (README.md:460, alongside the matching "No sub-agents / No plan mode / No MCP" not-built list). Stock pi therefore cannot produce the `working`-past-turn-end arc the scenario requires; a user who wants a detached process is told to run it under tmux out-of-band, where it is a separate OS process pi never tracks.

## Reasoning — daemon_capability: n/a; driver_capability: n/a

Because the agent lacks the feature, there is nothing to observe and no recording is producible — hence `n/a` rather than `no`. Independently, the transport could not observe it even if pi had it: the pi parser (`core/adapters/inbound/agents/pi/parser.go`) handles only `session`, `model_change`, `thinking_level_change`/`branch_summary`/`custom`, `compaction`, and `type:message` (roles assistant/user/toolResult/bashExecution). It has no `backgroundTaskId` extraction and emits no `BackgroundProcessCount` contribution — the entire #450/#445 mechanism (structured `backgroundTaskId` + `lsof` liveness probe gating `IsAgentDone`) is claudecode-specific and not wired for the pi `FilesUnderRoot` source.

## STRUCTURAL CONCERN — undefined requires-key (matrix applicability is definitionally undefined)

This scenario declares `requires: [tool_calls, background_process]` in `scenarios.json`. The capability id **`background_process` does NOT exist** in `replaydata/agents/features.json` (whose `features` map has no such key — it has `tool_calls`, `subagents`, `permission_hooks`, etc. but nothing for background processes), nor is there a `background_process` key in `replaydata/agents/pi/capabilities.json`. Until the maintainer either (a) adds the `background_process` capability id to `features.json` (and declares it per-adapter) or (b) corrects the `requires` array on the scenario, the matrix *applicability* of this cell is **definitionally undefined** — the requires-resolution can't be evaluated. The underlying-behavior verdict above stands regardless of how that key question is resolved: pi does not support background processes either way.

## Drift vs. prior matrix

Coverage row currently says `agent_supports: yes`, `daemon_capability: n/a; driver_capability: n/a` (notes empty). This assessment ratifies the column reassessment's flip: the prior `yes` overstated agent support (pi has no background-bash primitive at all). Corrected verdict is `no` / `n/a` (`applicable: no`). The maintainer should update the coverage row.
caveats: Undefined requires-key: scenario `requires: [tool_calls, background_process]` references capability id `background_process` which is absent from replaydata/agents/features.json and from pi's capabilities.json — matrix applicability for this cell is definitionally undefined until the maintainer adds the key or fixes the requires array. The behavior verdict (no) holds regardless.

`detached: true` appears in pi's bash tool source but is process-group hygiene for killProcessTree on timeout/abort, not a model-facing run_in_background feature — the bash tool still blocks on child exit and exposes no background parameter.
If a user runs a detached process via tmux (pi's documented workaround) it is a separate OS process pi never tracks in its transcript, so it is invisible to the pi adapter regardless.
Even hypothetically, the pi parser has no backgroundTaskId / BackgroundProcessCount path; the #450 lsof-probe mechanism is claudecode-specific.

sources.note: v0.70.0 bash tool: bashSchema has only {command, timeout}; execute() awaits waitForChildProcess(child) to completion (synchronous); detached:true is paired with trackDetachedChildPid/killProcessTree for process-group teardown, not backgrounding — no run_in_background param.
Line 460: 'No background bash. Use tmux. Full observability, direct interaction.' — explicit not-built disclaimer (v0.70.0).

ParseLine handles only session/model_change/thinking_level_change/branch_summary/custom/compaction/message(roles assistant,user,toolResult,bashExecution) — no backgroundTaskId extraction, no BackgroundProcessCount contribution.
Source: FilesUnderRoot + ExactName{pi} — observes a local transcript + local PID only; the background-process arc the spec asserts is the claudecode #450 mechanism, not wired here.

features map has no `background_process` key (only tool_calls/subagents/permission_hooks/etc.) — the scenario's requires-key is undefined; matrix applicability undefined until maintainer adds it or fixes requires.
No `background_process` key present for pi; cost_tracking=false, subagents=false, permission_hooks=false — confirms the requires-key is unmapped at the adapter level too.
pi design-rationale blog post (linked from README) framing pi as a local, fully-observable terminal agent with deliberately-omitted features.
